data.title: Quarterly Earnings: Signal Vs. Noise, Cost Vs. Benefit
data.description:
By Marc Fandetti, CFA

With the White House downplaying the value of quarterly reporting for companies, investors face a familiar question: does the cost of producing information outweigh the benefits?

Using Robert Shiller’s long-run data, this post shows that quarterly earnings contain information that is likely valuable to both long-term allocators and short-term traders. Its benefits, which I don’t attempt to quantify, should be weighed against any savings from less-frequent reporting.

Quarterly vs. Semi-Annual: What’s at Stake

The White House this week called for a change from quarterly to semi-annual earnings reporting. President Donald Trump argued that such a shift would save companies money and time.

That may be true. But would investors lose valuable information?

To answer this question, I use earnings data from Robert Shiller’s online data from January 1970 (1970:1), the year in which the Securities and Exchange Commission made quarterly earnings mandatory, to 2025:6 to test relationships among the change in three-month earnings, six-month earnings, and the trend in earnings. I define the trend as a 61-month centered moving average change in earnings. Specifically, I test whether knowing three-month earnings’ changes helps an investor better estimate changes in the longer-term trend in earnings.

Chart 1 shows three-month earnings in green, six-month earnings in red, and trend earnings in blue. Series start in January 2000 (2000:1), rather than 1970:1, for ease of visualization.

Chart 1. 3-month, 6-month, and trend earnings, 2000:1 to 2025:6.

Of course, three-month earnings are choppier than six-month earnings. But it is not obvious from visual inspection that knowing three-month earnings in addition to six-month earnings would help a long-term investor predict changes in trend earnings. (I test this below and find that they may).

It is, however, obvious that a short-term investor, one perhaps interested in earnings changes in periods of less than a year, would benefit from knowing three-month earnings. This observation is confirmed empirically below.

I start with the long-term investor, who I assume is interested in the long-term trend in earnings. A natural way to gauge the value of having three-month earnings in addition to (or instead of) six-month earnings is to model the change in trend earnings as a function of one or both, estimate that model using ordinary least squares, and compare model accuracy. In this post, I use R-squared as my measure of fit (or adjusted R-squared) — the larger, the better.

At any point, the investor knows one-half the current trend in earnings. That is, they know the first 30 months’ earnings of the current 61-month window, my proxy for the trend in earnings. And they know either the last three months of earnings, or the last six months of earnings, or both.

To determine whether receiving earnings information every three months as opposed to every six months would help the long-term investor to better predict the trend, I estimated specifications where the change in 30-month-ahead trend inflation is explained by the change in six-month earnings alone plus the prior earnings-trend change (Model 1). In Model 2, the trend change is explained by the same variables plus the three-month change in earnings. Results are shown in Table 1.

Table 1. Regressions of trend inflation change on 3- and 6-month earnings changes, 1970:1 – 2025:6.

Dependent variable = Trend inflation (30-month lead)
Model 1 Model 2
Six-mo. change (three-mo. lag) 0.073 (0.013) 0.061 (0.013)
Three-mo. change – 0.124 (0.029)
Trend change -0.223 (0.041) -0.234 (.040)
Adjusted R-squared 0.098 0.126
Obs 547 547

Source: Robert Shiller online data, author calculations.

Since I’m not interested in inference, I omit discussion of estimated coefficient values, other than to note that they enter with the expected sign. Notwithstanding this, I include the prior trend in earnings to reduce bias in my estimates, and standard errors appear in parentheses next to each estimate.

The key result is that adding quarterly earnings (three-month change) improves fit — the adjusted R-squared increases from 0.098 for Model 1 to 0.126 for Model 2. While neither fit is impressive, these results suggest that quarterly earnings may help the long-term investor predict trend earnings. Other measures of fit, namely the Akaike and Bayesian information criteria (AIC and BIC), confirm that the specification that includes 3-month earnings is more accurate.

As for what may be of interest to traders (short-term investors), one might guess that the three-month earnings change is related to the next three-month change. Quarterly earnings changes are indeed persistent. The scatter in Chart 2 shows the autocorrelation of quarterly earnings, where extreme values (earnings changes greater than 100%) have been removed for easier viewing. The estimated slope is 0.601 (se = 0.031) — the blue best-fit line is flatter than the black 45-degree diagonal line — and the R-squared is 0.361.

Chart 2. Three-month lagged earnings change vs. three-month earnings change, 1970:1 – 2025:6.

And at the risk of estimating the obvious, the R-squared of a model explaining 12-month earnings with six-month earnings (from six months before) is 0.699, whereas including three-month earnings (from three months before) improves the fit to 0.953.

Cost vs. Benefit

It is nearly axiomatic that, in most applications, more data is preferable to less. And the results discussed here suggest that quarterly earnings contain valuable information for investors. But producing earnings is costly.

As regulators consider reducing reporting frequency, they should weigh not just the savings but also the potential losses — losses to investors resulting from less transparency and to the economy resulting from impaired market efficiency.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bullish: Strong Bet On Institutional Crypto Growth
data.description:
Bullish (NYSE:BLSH) recently completed a hot IPO, making the stock difficult to value. The institutional crypto exchange owns both the Bullish Exchange and the CoinDesk brand, with major catalysts for entering the U.S. crypto exchange business and launching crypto options trading. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock after reporting mixed Q2 results, though Bullish is definitely a very risky play.

Don't Get Lost On Weak Q2

Bullish just went public back in August, raising nearly $1.3 billion by selling 34.5 million shares at $37. The company is run by Tom Farley, a former NYSE CEO, providing a top executive to traverse the regulatory environment.

The crypto exchange reported a generally weak Q2 '25 due to lower crypto trading volumes as follows:

The company reported Q2 sales actually slumped 6% YoY to only $57 million. Bullish saw the adjusted crypto transaction revenue dip to only $24.1 million, down from $34.9 million last Q2.

The Bullish Exchange saw trading volumes dip to the $50 billion range in the months from June through August. The company started the year with $80 billion in monthly crypto volumes even with Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) prices up during this period.

Bullish saw solid subscription revenue growing 27% to $32.9 million, but the investment story is really about the future growth in new products. The company just announced the approval of the BitLicense allowing Bullish to provide digital asset spot trading and custody services in New York state.

The company has a diverse revenue model via the Bullish Exchange and Liquidity Services along with various CoinDesk products. The business is mostly focused on crypto transaction fees and subscription services for Bullish Liquidity Services and CoinDesk Data.

The BitLicense allows Bullish to start U.S. operations on September 22. The company guided up to strong Q3 numbers based on a big boost to Subscription revenues jumping to the $45 million range, up from only $34 million in the prior quarter.

The U.S. crypto business will boost the Q4 results, especially due to the institutional focus that takes a while to launch and ramp up. In addition, Bullish is still working on launching the crypto options trading, leading to another major catalyst for the business heading into 2026.

The company focuses on institutional services and Coinbase has a report showing 83% of institutional investors plan to increase exposure to crypto. The rise of stablecoins after the U.S. government passed the GENIUS Act should drive adoption into 2026 with a platform like Bullish providing the opportunity for institutions to expand crypto exposure.

More Upside

The stock is up over 10% following the mixed Q2 results due to the strong Q3 guidance. The big question with any new IPO is gauging where the business is going in the future.

After the IPO, Bullish has a lot of excess capital. The company estimates the NAV amounts to nearly $3.6x billion with the current market cap of ~$9 billion.

The stock valuation definitely appears stretched with quarterly revenues of only $57 million. The key is the guidance and the expectations for 2026 with the launch of the U.S. crypto markets and options trading.

Bullish guided to Q3 revenues of $70+ million, so the company is getting to the annualized pace of $300 million before even adding the U.S. business. The consensus analyst estimates forecast 2026 revenues of only $307 million.

If Bullish can't vastly top these revenue targets, the stock should be avoided. The company isn't likely to add U.S. crypto operations and options trading to only generate minimal sequential growth in 2026 from running at the $300 million rate in Q3 '25.

The company already forecasts a large EBITDA profit in Q3 at $25+ million highlighting a highly profitable business model before even reaching scale. The stock would trade at about 90x the adjusted EBITDA run rate for the quarter while having a whole magnitude of catalysts, including the general adoption of crypto assets by institutions.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Bullish was a far better deal buying prior to the bump following Q2 earnings, but the stock likely has far more upside in the future. The business has multiple major growth catalysts heading into 2026 beyond just general crypto growth.

Investors should own the stock around $60, with the expectation for the company to sail past current consensus estimates. Naturally, the risk is Bullish failing to report enough growth to warrant a stock worth $8.7 billion.

data.title: CII: Strong Total Returns For Long-Term Investors And Monthly Pay
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund (NYSE:CII) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a monthly distribution through gains in its underlying portfolio and writing options. The fund has seen its discount narrow since our last update, helping to push total returns relatively higher on a share price basis. As the discount narrows, it can make it less appealing to consider a new investment aggressively. However, the fund isn't overly expensive either and can still be a solid long-term hold.

CII Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 2.48
  • Discount/Premium: -4.02%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.61%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.93%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $954 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

CII has a simple objective and strategy. It “seeks to provide investors with a combination of current income and capital appreciation” by “investing in a portfolio of equity securities of the U.S. and foreign issuers.” They will also “employ a strategy of writing call and put options.”

As a brief reminder, and as we noted in our prior update, the fund had a change in its name and investment policy.

Since our last update, the fund has also changed its name from the BlackRock Enhanced Capital and Income Fund under the same ticker. This change took place at the start of 2025. Along with the name change, the fund's investment policy also changed.

In connection with the name change, the Board has approved the adoption of a non-fundamental investment policy to invest at least 80% of the Fund’s net assets plus the amount of any borrowings for investment purposes, in large cap equity securities and derivatives that provide investment exposure to such securities or to one or more market risk factors associated with such securities (the “80% Policy”). For purposes of the 80% Policy, large cap equity securities are equity securities that at the time of purchase have a market capitalization within the range of companies included in the Russell 1000® Index.

That said, this actually wasn't a meaningful change in the policy, but to actually help better reflect how the fund invests. This also relates to new amendments in the Investment Company Act of 1940.

After careful review of the Fund’s current investment strategies and portfolio holdings, the Fund’s investment adviser, BlackRock Advisors, LLC (“BlackRock”), recommended that the Board approve the name change and adoption of the 80% Policy in order to comply with recent amendments to Rule 35d-1 under the Investment Company Act of 1940 (often referred to as the “Names Rule”) that will go into effect in 2025 and expand the scope of the Names Rule.

Performance - Discount Narrows

The fund has provided strong total returns since our prior update, which our last focused piece was right after the tariff announcement plunge, bringing the entire equity market down. As CII is an equity fund that invests somewhat similarly to the S&P 500 Index, this fund also saw a significant decline. However, as the broader market recovered and then some to push to new all-time highs, CII also recovered.

As a call writing fund, some upside can be limited during strong runs, such as what we saw. That said, CII overwrites individual names within its portfolio, and it is around 55% overwritten. That means a rather significant portion of the portfolio will not be limited to an upside cap. That can help during these sharp market recoveries compared to a fund that overwrites 100% of its portfolio.

Driving the bulk of those total return results was the recovery in the underlying portfolio of the fund. However, some of it also came from the fact that the fund saw its discount narrow a touch during this time—going from closer to a 7% discount to only around 4% today. That would have helped contribute to the fund's total share price return a bit more during this period. It is not trading at a slightly narrower discount compared to its longer-term history.

The fund uses the MSCI USA Call Overwrite Index and the Russell 1000 Index as its benchmarks, and we can see that it has outperformed the overwrite index in all time frames. On the other hand, it has struggled to keep up with the straight Russell 1000 Index. That goes back to the overwrite portion of the portfolio, with covered calls limiting some upside during strong market periods.

We've been pretty spoiled overall with strong equity gains, considering the last 10 years show over 13% annualized returns, and the last three years are over 16% for the Russell 1000 Index.

That does bring up the other point that this fund could face headwinds as the overall equity market, primarily those indexes that represent an outsized exposure to the technology sector and, more specifically, the Magnificent 7. That also includes the Russell 1000 Index, which isn't as concentrated as the S&P 500 Index or Nasdaq Composite, but is still tilted toward those same names.

Distribution - Monthly Pay

For income-focused investors, a monthly distribution can add quite a bit of appeal relative to quarterly payouts. Of course, there are now quite a few funds in the ETF space that have started to pay weekly distributions. While that can provide even faster compounding for those reinvesting, I believe that the frequency of the distribution isn't necessarily the most important question—monthly is still going to be completely fine for most investors.

For CII, the fund's distribution rate comes to 7.61%. The NAV distribution rate looks rather reasonable at 7.28% as well, which I believe is a more important question compared to the frequency. That can help us determine whether something is likely to be more sustainable or not. In general, a higher distribution rate generally means that a fund will see NAV/share price erosion over time—a distribution rate under 8% often indicates a reasonable level where NAV erosion would be expected to be limited or not see any erosion at all.

The caveat is that this is a constantly moving metric subject to the natural gyrations and whims of the markets' overall movements. As an equity-focused fund, the distribution is largely going to be derived from capital gains for CII. Those can come from the underlying portfolio or writing options and collecting the premiums. As of their last semi-annual report, the fund reported a net investment loss of a penny. Which means that CII's payout relied entirely on capital gains.

In the six months, the options written produced a realized loss as well, which means the entire distribution would have relied on the capital gains from the underlying portfolio. During the period, there was no shortage of that either, as they realized over $92 million and paid out ~$35 million for the six months.

Selling options can generate losses when they are closed out or rolled for more than the premiums received. However, that would generally only occur when the stock rises above the strike price the managers selected. By closing out the position, the loss realized on the options can be made up for with unrealized or realized appreciation due to a higher share price.

CII's Portfolio

CII has seen a rather meaningful shift in its portfolio since our last update, that has pushed it more toward the technology sector. Previously, we saw it was around 24% of the fund, but they've raised that now to nearly 36%. Going heavier or allowing a heavier tech allocation since late February, when we last saw the updated sector allocations, is an interesting choice.

It certainly paid off, as tech was the relatively weaker sector at the start of the year and into April. Those Mag 7 names largely drove the recovery and pushed the broader equity market to new all-time highs, and that would have benefited the fund quite a bit. However, going back to what looks like historically stretched valuations for the market, I'd start to wonder if having such a high allocation to tech makes sense for now.

On the other hand, it is where most of the growth in earnings is coming from, the Mag 7 names, which means some of the higher relative valuations can be justified. The fund is also overwritten by 55%, which is outside of its 30% to 40% overwrite range "under normal market conditions." Being more overwritten would indicate that management is perhaps more bearish.

In looking at the top 10 names, we see primarily the same names we saw earlier in the year. So while the sector allocations became more concentrated, the top 10 didn't necessarily shift too much. In fact, the top 10 represent a rather concentrated 53.54%, but that isn't too much of a dramatic shift from the 50.74% allocation previously.

In total, the fund lists 32 holdings, which isn't much for an equity fund—particularly one that is listed as a diversified equity fund, but that's more related to the sector allocation being rather diverse rather than the number of holdings. Over 35% in one sector is a high allocation, but it still leaves plenty of exposure elsewhere for it to be considered diversified.

With that said, CII is looking more like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) in terms of its sector allocation these days, as SPY carries around 35% in technology as well. For comparison, the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), an ETF representation of CII's listed benchmark, has a technology sector weighting of 31.72%. So it too is looking quite tech-heavy these days.

Conclusion

We are looking pretty stretched in valuations relative to historical levels. During April, the market pulled back and created a buying opportunity, but since then, we've rallied right back to overvalued levels. That's also besides CII's own discount, which looks rather shallow these days. I believe a long-term investor can continue to "Hold" here or even accumulate in smaller chunks; however, before buying more aggressively, I'd personally wait for an overall market correction. That could result in CII's discount widening back out during the volatility and create an even better entry price.

data.title: Lyft: Scaling Up, Globally
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lyft (NASDAQ:LYFT) is one of the leaders in the vibrant, evolving ride-sharing industry. Having previously operated only in the US and Canada, they announced expansion to Europe just a couple of months ago. While the US ride-sharing market has been in duopoly mode, the European market is more fragmented, and Lyft will face a bit of a different playground there.

Lyft’s financials have dramatically changed over the past two to three years. Having been a kind of symbol of “growth at any cost” since launching in 2012, burning scary amounts of cash, they have printed 7 consecutive quarters of positive cash flow as of today. In addition, the last quarter saw the first significant amount, $237 million, of stock buybacks.

The recent announcements of cooperation with May Mobility to launch robotaxis in Atlanta and cooperation with Baidu in Europe reflect a strategic focus to rise from just a great ride-hailing domestic clear second in the US to a leading global player involved in AVs and other related Mobility on Demand segments.

The ridesharing market is part of a larger Mobility on Demand market, which is expected to grow at a CAGR of 10% until 2035.

According to Statista, the revenue in the ride-hailing segment within the broader MOD market is expected to reach $179.70 billion by this year, and the annual growth rate (CAGR 2025-2030) of 5.06% is projected to result in a market volume of $229.98 billion by 2030 worldwide. In terms of user numbers, the ride-hailing market is expected to see a significant increase, with the number of users projected to reach 2.34 billion by 2030. In addition, the user penetration rate is projected to be 23.5% in 2025, while it is expected to reach 28.7% by 2030.

Uber (UBER), the absolute global leader in the ridesharing industry, is leading the innovation across multiple segments of the on-demand mobility ecosystem in addition to ride-hailing. It has food delivery (Uber Eats) and emerging services like grocery and freight services. In addition, both Uber and Lyft monetize their platform for advertising as well.

From March 2023, when David Risher was named CEO of Lyft, Lyft has received more positive spotlight, and while fixing the financials, they have been the first to innovate in many market-leading products. Price Lock, Silver, and Women+ Connect are a few to mention.

While Lyft was once a leader in the so-called free money era of creating growth at any cost, they have become a more modest but wisely growing company with leading innovations and solid financials as of today.

I rate Lyft as a Strong Buy and see a justified share price at least 25-30% higher at the $25-30 level.

Brief Overview

Lyft was founded back in 2012 by then computer programmers Logan Green and John Zimmer, who were behind the service named Zimride. It was originally meant as an intercity carpooling company focused on college transport. The story of the foundation tells that everything began after Green shared rides from the University of California, Santa Barbara campus to visit his girlfriend in Los Angeles while seeking an easier way to share rides.

The company changed its name to Lyft back in 2013. After years of incredible growth, in March 2019, Lyft made an IPO, raising $2.34 billion, bringing Lyft's valuation to $24.3 billion. The cash received gave them a nice pathway to grow the business even more rapidly. Revenues increased tenfold from $343 million in 2016 to $3.616 billion in 2019 when COVID-19 hit the economy. Alongside revenue expansion, the annual net loss hit a record, reaching $2.602 billion in the very same all-time high revenue year.

The pandemic was an extraordinarily tough time for any kind of public transport, but ride-hailing companies maneuvered the situation relatively well and got their restrictions softened earlier. The industry started to fully come back to normal sooner than other transportation services.

Lyft’s road from loss leader to financially responsible company wasn’t easy and smooth. As the 2020 earnings print showed, the revenue fell around 35% that year, to $2.365 billion. The loss was remarkable: 74% of revenue, -$1.753 billion. The company needed to take on the $734 million of long-term debt to get over tough times.

The following years were full of different headwinds, and the progress of making the company profitable was relatively modest. In the middle of 2022, the company's share price also plummeted to an all-time low and remained hovering between $10 and $20. 2023 saw the stock breaking under the $10 mark multiple times and staying there for some periods.

While "restructuring" and "effectiveness" were the most used words in earnings calls and at analyst presentations since 2020, in my view, one could say that with the appointment of David Risher in March 2023, the new era of Lyft started.

Recent Developments

In April 2023, after a month of David Rishner's appointment, the company announced layoffs of 1,076 corporate workers, or 26% of its staff. This came in addition to job cuts previously announced in July and November 2022.

In September 2023, Lyft discontinued Lyft Rentals and also stopped offering car rental services. In the second quarter of 2024, Lyft posted its first-ever GAAP profit in the company’s history, making the symbolic mark of successful transformation from growth at any price to a full-grown, solid, strong-footed company with well-balanced but still really attractive growth prospects.

Second Quarter Earnings

The last quarter, while missing revenue estimates by just $10 million, was overall impressive. Gross bookings set another record high at $4.5 billion, up 12% year over year, and marked the 10th consecutive quarter of records. Revenue was $1.6 billion, up 11.2% yoy. Active riders posted a 9th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth at 10% yoy to $26.1 million, exceeding analysts' estimates by 240K. The growth of rides was 14% yoy and jumped to 234.8 million, although less than the 236.05 million analysts had expected.

Profitability beat Wall Street’s expectations as unadjusted earnings increased to $0.10 per share, from $0.01 in the same quarter last year, beating the $0.04 per share expectation. Adjusted EBITDA of $129.4 million was an all-time high, increasing by 26% year-over-year. In addition, Lyft generated a record free cash flow of $329.4M. For the trailing 12 months ending in June, free cash flow reached close to the $1 billion mark, printed at $993.0 million.

Looking ahead, Lyft expected gross bookings to increase between 13% and 17%, to $4.65 billion and $4.8 billion, which exceeded the $4.6 billion estimate. Adjusted EBITDA was projected between $125 million and $145 million, with margins of 2.7% and 3.0%.

While quantitative metrics were strong, the same was true for other aspects of the Lyft report:

Still shares were under pressure after the earnings release, partly blamed on the announcement preceded by Uber in the same morning, which also came a bit weaker than expected, having set the mood for the whole sector accordingly. Lyft's slight revenue and ride miss was also cited as a cause of the share's reaction.

Lyft's CEO, David Rishner, was confident in the earnings call:

“We delivered off-the-charts performance, resulting in our strongest quarter ever” “Our marketplace is thriving, our TAM is expanding with the close of FREENOW, and we are building meaningful partnerships,”

He specifically highlighted the new partnership with United Airlines (UAL), which allows MileagePlus members to earn miles on all Lyft rides.

In addition, strategic partnerships with DoorDash (DASH) and Hilton (HLT) were highlighted, together with expansion into autonomous driving with Baidu (BIDU), Marubeni, May Mobility, and Mobileye (MBLY), which were also outlined as major accomplishments.

Presentation at Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference

On September 9, 2025, Erin Brewer, Chief Financial Officer of Lyft, participated in the conference call at the Goldman Sachs conference.

I made a summary of the most remarkable highlights in my view as follows.

Lyft's CFO, Erin Brewer, reminded that 2.5 years ago, about 8 weeks after joining the company, it was his first investor conference, making the presentation special to highlight the journey that Lyft has been on. 2.5 years ago, they were largely North America-focused, not a profitable company, burning cash, and playing a little bit of catch-up. Today, they have built an incredible foundation, strong growth across the rider base, driver hours, and a strong foundation of profitability.

Erin Brewer followed with remarks about the acquisition of Freenow and the autonomous partnerships Lyft has announced. In Lyft's management view, they are defining what it means today to be a really global mobility platform with multiple engines for growth.

Erin also talked about not only growing across the markets where they are already strong but also what they call underpenetrated markets. These are in large part outside of the top cities and represent 2/3 of the TAM that exists across North America. The CFO sees it as a tremendous opportunity for Lyft to continue to grow and highlighted growth in cities like Charlotte and Indianapolis that are growing at rates above 30%.

Further, Erin outlined their strong product innovation as being a category leader in the novel products that they have introduced to the market: Price Lock, driver earnings commitment, Silver, Women+ Connect, plus everything that does a great job engaging new categories of riders. He was really confident that their lead in product innovation continues alongside partnerships, which are a really important pillar. The company is super excited to add United Airlines to its partnerships list and expects to launch new products with partners in the upcoming quarters. Erin Brewer highlighted that in Q2 alone, 50 million rides were attached to some collaboration.

The CFO admits a very strong partnerships portfolio for Lyft, but it's still underpenetrated in the company's view, as none of those are more than 20% penetrated. Lyft management sees the company having a runway to continue to drive strong active rider growth while also growing ride frequency.

The additional topics discussed were pricing strategies alongside competitive dynamics. Erin Bewer pointed out that the concept of affordability is in Lyft's DNA, giving the example of a great product called the Wait & Save, where riders trade time for price. With the same focus, they service customers all the way through the highest-value modes of product offerings. They have seen pretty strong growth on the high end and believe that this proves they have a really customer-obsessed mindset around affordability.

Erin Bewer also made an example of Price Lock as a really interesting product, while not necessarily at its core, around affordability. It really strengthens a use case around high-frequency riders who want to have pricing predictability. The company management thinks that while generally Lyft operates in a competitive market, it's a pretty stable environment overall. Their competitiveness is supported by being at the leading edge of that innovation, and they do it in an exceptionally thoughtful way.

Another talking point that got attention was related to insurance. That has been a really important topic on their road to getting a profitable company, as these insurance costs have been a very significant part of the overall cost base. Erin Bewer was confident that the changes made in the insurance program over the last couple of years are resilient and the multiyear strategic plan on that front will be successful going ahead.

In addition, the CFO mentioned that in the first half of 2025, the CPI for auto insurance inflation is in the high single-digit range, and that's the backdrop with which they are negotiating and looking forward to the next renewal. The company is highly confident about executing this in line with, or slightly better than, the observed inflation trends in the industry.

In the important AV discussion, Lyft's CFO presented the company’s view of AV as a very exciting opportunity for them and for the industry overall. Erin Bewer discussed how fascinating it is to see a handful of cities in the U.S., where some portion of AVs on the road are growing at 5x the rate of other equivalent cities that don't have those deployments. He pointed out that it is not about coming into a city and kind of taking from a pie that is fixed and exists, but rather the data actually tells us that these AVs on the roads expand the use cases overall.

The Lyft management sees that AVs' expansion is developing as a hybrid network over time, where AVs are well-positioned to execute effectively against predictable routes and in more high-frequency routes. At the same time, the industry will absolutely have human drivers that are engaged during times of surge, where there's an event letting out, where there are very complex routes or pick-ups and drop-offs, or where that human touch on a ride, whether it's luggage assistance or otherwise, is just essential.

CFO concludes that the AVs sector is still in early stages and has a lot of companies out there piloting, experimenting, and Lyft is participating in that. Their roadmap includes May Mobility cooperation upcoming in 2025, followed by other AV opportunities with Baidu, Benteler, Mobileye, and Marubeni across 2026.

About the acquisition of Freenow, the CFO said that they see it as one element of the expansion of Lyft's global platform. The deal was, in management's view, a very strategic and efficient way to enter a major market. With Freenow, they got entry into 9 countries. While it’s a good little business in and of itself, they saw the opportunity even organically to make it better, to raise the bar overall on service, to take some of the innovations from existing businesses and take them across to Freenow.

In addition, the CFO pointed out the case of helping with the acquisition to strengthen their partnership ecosystem. All of their partners, Hilton, Chase, DoorDash, and Alaska Air, are international, and it makes Lyft a much more interesting global partner for rider-facing partnerships. They see themselves as a much more interesting partner for autonomous collaborations, also. In summary, globalizing the platform makes Lyft more interesting to global brands that engage with the company today while operating at a global scale.

On the financial side, Erin Bewer mentioned that Freenow tends to carry a higher average gross booking per ride compared to their existing North America-focused business. From a revenue margin standpoint, it carries a pretty different revenue margin characteristic, being in the low teens compared to the classic North American businesses that are in the mid-30%.

In finalizing remarks, Lyft's CFO Erin Bewer highlighted the importance of partnerships, especially the one with DoorDash, which has been an exceptionally strong partnership for them, offering a strong value proposition. The growth rate of riders that DoorDash has brought into Lyft's ecosystem has continued to grow at a faster rate than the overall business. They see it as just about 10% penetrated, with therefore impressive growth ahead.

In addition, the CFO of Lyft offered some final remarks about Lyft Media's ad business. They are on track to print a $100 million booking rate by the end of 2025. Having brought in a new leader of that business, someone who is extremely steep in ads and management expects that she will continue to provide strong fuel for growth. They continue to grow in-app ads across global brands, which continue to come back to the platform. The CFO made an example about a campaign with Sephora where the digital and the real world come together and drive really strong foot traffic.

Valuation

From the financial and valuation estimates, there was nothing new in the CFO's remarks at the Goldman Sachs conference. Erin Bewer was overall notably upbeat and sees Lyft as better positioned than ever before. The CFO pointed to about under $1 billion of free cash flow in the trailing 12 months and mentioned that the previously talked about adjusted EBITDA to free cash flow conversion in 2025 would be similar to 2024. Even looking into 2026, Lyft's management thinks the outcome is going to be well above the latest June 2024 Investor Day targets.

Lyft's management is very confident about its prospects, and it seems to me that while it's appropriate to stay with estimated financials, they could potentially surprise positively in upcoming earnings if the broader economy avoids major disruptions.

I think Lyft is quite significantly undervalued both in absolute and relative terms. Having a 2027 expected forward P/E ratio at around level 11-12 is a strong confirmation of that. The P/E ratios of this level would be normal in cases of mature, so-called “cash cow” no-growth type of dividend payers, but not in the case of a company acting in one of the most compelling and vibrant marketplaces in the world with significant growth ahead.

If we look at the relative value standpoint, comparing Lyft with other Mobility on Demand sector companies, the outcome confirms the significant undervaluation:

Company Name

P/S 2027

P/E 2027

Dif % P/S

Dif % P/E

LYFT

1

11.7

-68%

-49%

UBER

3.05

GRAB

70%

DIDI Global

0.85

Instacart/Maplebear

2.71

-20%

3.13

22.75

Source: Author's creation

In my view, given the strong and growing financials and overall growth of the sector, Lyft is significantly undervalued and, even conservatively, taking into account potential macro headwinds, worth at least 25%-30% more than the current share price level in the $20s.

Risks

The main risk is certainly autonomous vehicles. It's not Lyft's specific risk, but the same for all industry current leaders. As Lyft's CFO discussed in the Goldman Sachs conference, they don’t see it this way and even look at it as an opportunity. I totally agree with this and think that the playground will evolve mixed, like Lyft management also sees it. Lyft and Uber have a strong first-hand lead, or moat, considering all these nuanced platform integrations and regulations one could face trying to enter any segment of the ridesharing market.

In addition, as the Lyft CFO pointed out, they are innovating hand in hand with as many AV partners as possible, which, in my opinion, keeps them at a really good level of involvement if some of the AV innovations are getting too competitive and trying to grab market share independently. I don’t see that both Uber and Lyft would be left cold here; rather, on the contrary, they could be winners here through different partnerships and additional innovations.

The regulatory risks have always been one of the most challenging headwinds for the ride-hailing sector. As time goes on, the risk, in my view, has declined as people are taking ride-hailing as the new normal. The taxi drivers have also been adopting, as the strikes and protests against these new platforms haven’t been loudly in the newsfeeds for some time.

I believe that the globalization efforts actually lessen Lyft's risks broadly. Previously, the US government regulations and other potential US domestic sources of headwinds were concentrated; the global approach is positive from that viewpoint.

The other threats Lyft faces are the usual execution risks common to every corporation of this size. Such as maintaining pricing power amid macroeconomic and competitive headwinds, scaling successfully, integration headwinds, etc., which, in my view, are manageable and belong to management's daily execution to minimize these risks.

Takeaway

I see the Lyft narrative as an exciting turnaround story, having everything ready for the next phase of growth. While at the helm of founders, the company saw incredible growth from campus idea to national market leader; their fate is now in the hands of more balanced, strategically wise management. They are prepared to lead the company to become a leading player on a global scale in the dynamic and rapidly evolving Mobility on Demand sector.

GARP Competition

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bitcoin Extends Advance Near $118k After Fed Rate Cut
data.description:
By Sholanke Dele

​Bitcoin (BTC-USD) price extended its advance on Thursday, lifted by the Federal Reserve’s first interest rate cut since 2024. The decision to lower borrowing costs by a quarter point provided a modest boost across risk assets, and the global crypto market cap rose 2% to $4.2 trillion. Bitcoin held firm through the announcement and pushed toward $118,000, marking its highest level in five weeks.

The move came after Wednesday’s low near $114,700, which aligned with support at the 50 exponential moving average on the four-hour chart. That technical foundation encouraged dip buying, while a steady increase in open interest added fuel to the rally. By the late Asian session on Thursday, Bitcoin had touched a fresh high close to $118,000, surpassing the previous day’s peak and extending September’s uptrend.

Bitcoin price dynamic (Aug - Sept 2025). Source: TradingView

The fundamental backdrop was less straightforward. Fed Chair Jerome Powell described the cut as a risk management step rather than the start of an aggressive easing cycle. He stressed that policymakers would not rush into deeper cuts, a signal that tempered broader risk sentiment. Traders who had already anticipated a rate trim found little reason to drive a sharp rally across financial markets. Still, Bitcoin managed to sustain its upward trajectory, showing relative resilience compared to other assets.

Bitcoin RSI remains bullish, leaving room for further upside extension

At the start of the European session on Thursday, Bitcoin was trading near $117,200, up 0.6% on the day. The price action confirms that buyers continue to dominate the near-term structure, keeping momentum tilted higher. The ability to close above $118,000 would further strengthen bullish conviction, potentially clearing the path toward the $120,000 threshold. This level stands as the next psychological marker for traders watching Bitcoin’s expansion through September.

Technical conditions continue to support the outlook. The daily Relative Strength Index remains in bullish territory but has not yet crossed into overbought levels, leaving room for additional upside without immediate risk of exhaustion. Combined with steady participation through rising open interest, this indicates that buyers are still confident in testing higher ground.

Overall, Bitcoin’s resilience following the Fed decision tells the strength of its trend. While Powell’s cautious remarks limited enthusiasm across traditional risk assets, Bitcoin’s ability to maintain momentum suggests that the market is pricing in not just the current cut but the possibility of easier conditions through the months ahead. The near-term challenge lies in holding above $118,000 to confirm the breakout, after which $120,000 becomes the next target for traders.

Bitcoin traded above $116,800 as FOMC decision dominated market focus. At the same time, open interest rise confirmed capital inflows supporting the rally.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Hims & Hers Health: Bumpy Ride Will End
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) has hit a bumpy ride over the last year, with the stock stuck in a range from $40 to $70. The online health and wellness platform has faced a lot of questions regarding compounding GLP-1s, with the latest news being a letter from the FDA. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the stock, as the company moves forward with growth plans while the GLP-1 business was never the long-term focus.

FDA Questions

Hims dipped 6% on the U.S. FDA sending a letter to the company questioning the marketing of their GLP-1 compounds. The FDA had previously questioned the Super Bowl ad from Hims & Hers Health with Marty Makary, Commissioner of the U.S. FDA, pointing out the ad failed to mention side effects of compounded GLP-1 drugs.

The stock rebounded from larger losses as the CEO went on Twitter to claim the FDA sent similar letters to ~100 companies across the industry. Most importantly, big Pharma got those letters as well, so this isn't anything specific where Hims & Hers Health is doing something egregious with compounded GLP-1s.

As usual, the stock market sells first and asks questions later. Hims & Hers is down on fears the FDA is signaling out the online health platform when in reality the scrutiny of health regulators is likely to reduce a lot of the competition in the online health space.

On the Q2 earnings report, Hims again pointed out the weight-loss drug segment was targeted at a $725 million business in 2025, with GLP-1s accounting for a portion of that business. The company has forecast 2025 revenue of $2.35 billion, so the weight-loss segment is just 31% of total revenues.

The investment story was never based solely on compounded GLP-1 drugs and the partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO) never really added up, with the platform not really built for re-selling branded drugs. Hims did report Q2 revenue of $545 million, which dipped sequentially by $41 million due to the hiccup of transitioning patients off the commercially available GLP-1s, but the company is already back to reporting sequential growth with the Q3'25 target of at least $580 million.

Focus on Future

The market cap has fallen to an $11.5 billion market cap, with the stock only trading at 4x 2026 sales targets of just $2.8 billion. Hims will likely see solid gains on these targets once all of the noise around compounded GLP-1s leaves the story.

Hims just launched new offerings for low testosterone and is moving full-speed forward with at-home tests after acquiring a blood testing lab. The company sees a market opportunity in hormonal health of 50 million patients in the U.S. underrecognized by traditional healthcare.

The company just closed the Zava acquisition and targets a $50 million revenue potential for 2025. On the Q2'25 earnings call, the CEO outlined a large opportunity in the international markets despite investor concerns on government healthcare outside the U.S.:

Ultimately, we believe the international market is a focused effort, right? It's not a spray and pray model. We don't believe that there are dozens of markets that are required for a substantial revenue footprint. I think long term, we believe there's a multibillion-dollar revenue opportunity in just a handful of key markets. And I think you'll see us in the next 1 to 3 years going after those focused markets.

Hims remains a huge play on personalized services, but what the market has probably missed is that the online platform moved beyond solving just one medical condition. At the end of Q2, the company has nearly 500K subscribers utilizing personalized treatment plans to target multiple conditions, up nearly 170% YoY.

The online health and wellness platform continues to innovate by looking for solutions to problems not solved by the current medical system and has moved far beyond simple ED pills. The long-term goal is to turn Hims & Hers Health in a membership platform to cover the majority of medical conditions impacting patients, with the business transformed to the goal of preventing medical issues versus treating them.

The company has 2030 financial targets of at least $6.5 billion in revenue and $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA, but these are just stepping stones on the path to 10+ million subscribers. Hims obtains $74 per month from existing subscribers, and 10 million subs at this monthly revenue would generate $8.9 billion in annual revenues, far above current estimates.

The stock has been stuck in a range from $40 to $70 over most of the year. As the company returns to sequential sales growth and moves beyond this volatile GLP-1 period, Hims should trade higher.

Naturally, the risk is to run into problems with compounded GLP-1 sales. In addition, the online health and wellness platform has substantial competition that could slow down the growth of the business.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Hims trades at a cheap valuation despite explosive growth and huge catalysts for the next decade. The stock is down on the drama with GLP-1s, but the business is moving far beyond a focus on weight-loss drugs, making the recent weakness a buying opportunity.

data.title: Fed Pivots: What The Rate Cut Means For Bonds, Gold, And AI Stocks
data.description:
The Federal Reserve delivered the expected 25-basis-point rate cut. There is a clear shift in tone and direction. Especially in the Q&A, it sounded like a pivotal meeting. Powell appeared concerned about the job market and not very worried about inflation anymore.

Why isn't he worried about inflation when it is at 3% and has been there for a long time now? One answer is because the Fed views tariffs as a temporary influence that can’t be combatted by hiking rates. Another likely reason (which they aren't communicating in so many words) is because it sees the economy cooling off. I'll go over a few key quotes from the Fed's Q&A session. All emphasis has been put there by me.

A journalist asked him if the balance of risks was no longer pointing towards restrictive policy (if they aren't, they are pointing towards easing policy)

MR. POWELL: So I don’t – I don’t think we can say – we can say that. What we can say is this, that over the course of this year we’ve kept our policy at a restrictive level – and people have different views, but a clearly restrictive level, I would say. So – and we were able to do that over the course of this year because the labor market was in very solid condition with strong job creation and all those things.

I think if you go back to April and now look at the revised job creation numbers for May, June, July, and August, you can kind of – I can no longer say that. So what that means is that the risks, which – the risks were clearly tilted toward inflation. I would say they’re moving toward equality. Maybe they’re not quite at equality. We don’t need to know that. But we got to – we do know that they’ve moved meaningfully toward greater equality, the risks between the two goals, and that suggests that we should be moving in the direction of neutral. And that’s what we did today.

This is early in the Q&A when he's still trying to sound hawkish. But he gets asked about the jobs market repeatedly:

MR. POWELL: So it’s an interesting labor market. And obviously we think it’s appropriate that we reduce our rates, so that we become more neutral, which will be – which presumably will be better for the labor market. You see people who are sort of more at the margins – so kids coming out of college and younger people, minorities – are having a hard time finding jobs. The overall job finding rate is very, very low. However, the layoff rate is also very low. So you’ve got a low – a low firing, low hiring environment.

And the concern is that if you start to see layoffs, the people who are laid off won’t – there won’t be a lot of hiring going on. So that could very quickly flow into higher unemployment. In a healthier economy, healthier labor market, there would be jobs for those people. But now the hiring rate is very, very low. So that’s been a growing concern over the last few months. And it’s one of the reasons why we think it’s appropriate that we begin to shift our policy focus toward a more balanced one.

Powell also believes more restrictive immigration policies have resulted in lower labor supply. This seems to be backed by data indicating a rare decline in the U.S. foreign-born population. To an extent this balanced out a fall in labor demand.

The number of immigrants is obviously much lower than the number of employed people. If the demand for workers is declining, this can be counteracted by decreasing the inflow through immigration only to a limited extent. If demand for workers continues to decline, it becomes more obvious as this buffering force is gone.

Years prior, Powell had been very consistent in pointing out the danger of sticky inflation and reiterating the Fed wants to push it down. Even at times accepting that it would limit the buoyancy of the job market. Here’s how he explains why he’s switching gears:

Right now, the situation we’re in is that we see inflation – we continue to expect it to move up. Maybe not as high as we would have expected it to move up a few months ago. The pass through into – of the tariffs into inflation has been slower and smaller. The labor market has softened. So the case for there being a persistent inflation outbreak is less. And so that’s why we think it’s time for us really to acknowledge that the risks to the other mandate have grown, and that we should move in the direction of neutral. So what will we do? We’ll do what we need to do. But we have two mandates, and we try to balance them.

For a long time, our framework says that when our two goals are in tension – this is quite an unusual situation – how do we decide what to do? Because our tools can’t do two things at once. What we do is we ask, which is farther from – how far is each from the goal and how long is it expected to get to the goal? So and then that’s – we think about those things, and we see – as I mentioned, we have been – our policy had been really skewed toward inflation for a long time, really. Now we see that there’s downside risk clearly in the labor market. And so we’re moving in a direction of more neutral policy.

He even sounded somewhat defensive, justifying that it is only a 25bp cut by pointing out they could do more in the future:

So we put all this together and we see that that the labor market is softening. And we don’t need it to soften any more, don’t want it to. So we use our tools. And, you know, it starts with a 25 basis point rate cut, but we – you know, the market’s also pricing in a rate path. I’m not blessing what the market’s doing at all. I’m just saying it’s not just one action.

This went from "moving towards a more neutral stance" because the balance is tilting away from having to guard against inflation to "doing more in the future".

For the first time in a very long time, I think the 10-year treasury could be very attractive. The yield is above 4%. Employment data tends to trend. The 10-year doesn’t work when inflation surges upwards. However, it may not be bothered by tariff (temporary) related inflation impact. If the labor market continues to cool, this will impact growth. Which becomes a strong disinflationary force. The Fed appears ready and willing to cut into a 3%+ inflation scenario (depending on the underlying cause), and the administration is eager to appoint a dovish new chair in 2026. If the labor market/economy stabilizes, I’d be very worried about the push for dovish monetary policy. An example of an ETF benefitting from that trend is the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF)

Things continue to look great for gold. The price chart above shows the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) year-to-date result of 37.99%. A slowdown or recession isn't necessarily bad for gold. Even the truly bad scenario of stagflation isn’t bad for gold. It tends to be viewed as a safe haven asset. If bonds start doing really well (with clear disinflation), it could draw money away. The scenario where gold would be most likely to struggle—continued rate hikes by the Fed and the administration pushing for a strong dollar—seems quite far-fetched at this point.

Equities, exemplified by the S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY) and the dollar (USD), are starting to look like some of the more risky places. Counterintuitively, I expect some of the "hyped" AI hardware companies to perform relatively well. Think NVIDIA (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductors (TSM), etc.

An important way to get ahead in the race between models is by throwing increasingly vast amounts of compute towards the problem. That will continue to be a reliable way forward. Hopefully it gets more and more supported by more cost-efficient innovation, but it almost guarantees progress.

There’s a large advantage to having a top-tier model in terms of attracting customers, attracting funding, cost of capital, and getting broad data on how customers are interacting with these models. True, a recession could cut some of the contenders off from capital. At some point several players are going to drop out of the top-tier race anyway. However, in the near term I’d expect spending to remain very high. It is either spend or give up.

Some of the companies doing this spending are more vulnerable than they would otherwise be during a slowdown: Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META), among others. It's not that I would short them, but it is something to be aware of.

data.title: We Still Like Darden
data.description:
After a nice profitable trade on Darden Restaurants, Inc. (NYSE:DRI), we have been advocating maintaining a house position consisting of a portion of the profit for long-term returns. At BAD BEAT Investing, we love this approach to generational wealth creation. The beauty of holding a portfolio of hundreds of house positions in profitable trades is that it allows you to receive all future dividends, gains, spinoffs, etc. long-term, while allocating principal to other short-term trading opportunities. Rinse and repeat. Darden has been doing well since the trade, though it is pulling back some today following the just reported fiscal Q1 and the outlook.

Darden fiscal Q1 revenue

This was a missed quarter overall, with a very slight miss on revenues and a $0.04 miss on the EPS line. Let's start with sales. In the competitive restaurant sector, comparable sales are a key measure we continue to monitor for restaurants. In the just-reported fiscal Q1 2025, Darden did see growth in sales versus the prior year. Revenue was up 8.7% to $3.0 billion this quarter. As mentioned, it was a very slight miss versus consensus estimates by $40 million.

Darden fiscal Q1 comparable sales

How about the critical comparable sales figure? Well, they were up overall, with some mixed performance in fine dining. Comparable sales for the entire company were up 4.7%. This is big positive. When we dig into the comparable sales by each operating restaurant segment, we see that DRI's LongHorn Steakhouse continues to be the big winner, while Olive Garden really surged. Fine dining was the only main segment with comp declines, and so-called "other business" was up too.

Under the LongHorn Steakhouse banner, comparable sales were up a strong 5.5%. The Olive Garden banner led the way with comps rising 5.9%. The fine dining part of the business was once again a bit weak with comparable sales falling 0.2%. It would be great if they would break this out a bit more so we knew where there was strength and weakness within the line. The so-called “other business” rose 3.3%.

Rick Cardenas, Darden President & CEO, stated in the earnings release:

We had a strong start to the fiscal year with same-restaurant sales and earnings growth that exceeded our expectations. The strength of our results is a testament to the power of our strategy. Across our portfolio, our restaurant teams remained focused on being brilliant with the basics and, at the Darden level, we continued to leverage our four competitive advantages to position our brands for long-term success. Our winning strategy is enabling us to grow sales and market share, while making meaningful investments in our business and returning capital to our shareholders

While it was mixed it was a decent quarter overall, considering the economic mix and what we are seeing in jobs data. While it was mixed on some lines, the market is rewarding the stock. What about profit? There have been some margin concerns. Segment profit followed the sales trends. Olive Garden saw a profit of $267.6 million vs. $250.1 million last year. The LongHorn Steakhouse banner had a segment profit of $134.9 million, rising from $128.4 million a year ago. Fine dining profit was about flat at $38.7 million, versus $38.9 million a year ago, while the other business banners saw a nice 30% increase in profit, rising to $109.3 million. Factoring in expenses, Darden reported adjusted EPS of $1.97, which missed estimates by $0.04.

We also like the share repurchases and dividends. With this morning's dividend announcement of $1.50 per share for the quarter, the stock still provides a 3% yield with today's pull back. That is a 10 cent per quarter boost from the old dividend of $1.40 quarterly. We do expect ongoing dividend increases in the future. Darden bought back $183 million worth of shares in the quarter. At this level, we think the valuation is balanced for the growth here, in our opinion, justifying a comfortable hold rating.

The guidance was positive as well overall. It upped sales growth to 7.5% to 8.5% for the year, higher on the low end. Same store sales should grow 3% at the midpoint while new restaurant openings were upped to 65 openings from 60-65 previously. Further EPS should come in at $10.60 at the midpoint. At $190 per share this is pretty fair value for the moderate growth.

Final thoughts

We like Darden Restaurants, Inc.'s shareholder-friendly policies here with the dividend and buybacks, but view shares as pretty fairly valued. We continue to like holding a house position and collecting long-term returns and cash flow from the growing dividends.

data.title: TBIL: The Fed Has Cut, What Is Next (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The US Treasury 3-Month Bill ETF (NASDAQ:TBIL) is a $6 billion fund and a reference point in the short term treasury fund cohort. The instrument is one of the most utilized cash parking vehicles, and we have covered this ETF before with a 'Buy' rating.

With the much anticipated Fed meeting from September 17, 2025, now behind us and a 25 bps cut, we are going to revisit TBIL and outline for investors what the recent cut means.

Buying T-Bills via an ETF

As a reminder, TBIL strives to provide exposure to the current 3-month Treasury Bill and thus represents a play on the front end of the yield curve. Its composition is reflective of that:

Please note the ETF holds three issuances, with the maximum maturity being December 2025.

Retail investors have the ability to buy T-Bills outright, but they have to go through the operational process of rolling them upon expiration. TBIL does that for you automatically, although there is a slight tax tweak one has to be aware of. This writer learned it the hard way when doing taxes - while outright T-Bills are reported automatically by brokerage companies as having exempt interest from state and local taxes, ETFs like TBIL need to have a manual calculation that needs inputting into tax software. Outside this slight reporting tweak, the fund represents a similar exposure to just rolling 3-month T-Bills.

If we take a look at historic correlations, we can see the one-for-one impact when Fed Funds are lowered:

The blue line in the chart above represents the Federal Funds Effective Rate, while the green line is the 3-month constant maturity T-Bills. Note the almost perfect correlation, with very short periods of very slight yield discrepancies. In essence this graph tells us that as the Fed cuts, 3-month T-Bills will follow Fed Funds lower.

The Fed has cut; what is next?

The Fed disappointed some market participants by having only a 25 bps cut, but two more cuts are priced in for the rest of 2025:

WASHINGTON – The Federal Reserve on Wednesday approved a widely anticipated rate cut and signaled that two more are on the way before the end of the year as concerns intensified over the U.S. labor market even as inflation is still in the air.

In an 11-to-1 vote signaling less dissent than Wall Street had anticipated, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark overnight lending rate by a quarter percentage point. The decision puts the overnight funds rate in a range between 4.00%-4.25%.

Chairman Powell mentioned that the decisions in the past regarding 50 bps cuts or increases were driven by the narrative of the Fed being behind what they thought would be an appropriate monetary stance. The current macro framework was described as being balanced, with less labor available balancing out less job availabilities:

“The marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers is unusual in this less dynamic and somewhat softer labor market,” he said. “The downside risks to employment appear to have risen.”

The topic of inflation was also discussed, with the Fed base-case including a one-time price increase from tariffs, rather than continuous inflation causing price moves.

While we can discuss and debate where monetary policy should be, one thing is for certain, and that is what the market is now pricing in:

The above graph plots the futures market for 1-month SOFR and the Fed Dot Plot. While the 'Dot Plot' is subject to debate, change, and political nominations, the SOFR futures market is not. The market is showing us deep cuts to come in the next year, with 1-month SOFR at 3% come December 2026.

We stated that the Fed Dot Plot is subject to various political nominations because we saw an interesting development in the latest Fed release. While most of the projections were quite similar, there was a Fed member who plotted an astounding 125 bps of cuts for the rest of 2025:

Only one official called for no cuts, while another member of the FOMC thought the central bank should cut rates by an additional 1.25% percentage point by the end of 2025. Because the dot plot is anonymous we don’t know for sure which member of the FOMC made each of these predictions. But given calls by the Administration for a significant cut in interest rates, it seems likely it was made by one of the President’s recent appointments.

The takeaway here is that the 'Dot Plot' projections are fickle, and investors are better served by looking at what the futures market is telling us.

Impact to TBIL

TBIL will see its 30-day SEC yield decrease as per the latest cut and will continue to embed almost one-for-one any future rate decreases. If indeed Fed Funds are at 3% by the end of 2026, do expect TBIL to yield something very similar at that point in time.

TBIL is purely an automated way to get exposure to short-dated T-Bills and the front of the yield curve and thus represents an almost direct take on Fed Funds. Should we be happy that TBIL will yield less? No. Is it in-line with what the fund is supposed to do? Absolutely.

Investors looking for protection from decreasing Fed Funds should look for longer-duration names, and we have covered such funds in the past:

  • SHY: Smart Duration Play, 4.9% Yield
  • NEAR: Still A Buy After The Rise In Rates

While there are other short-term funds that offer higher yields, they do come with either asset default risk or equity risk. Investors looking for clean treasury exposure should look only at higher-duration names.

Conclusion

TBIL is a cash parking vehicle composed of T-Bills. With a negligible duration and an almost perfect correlation to Fed Funds, the ETF represents a take on the front end of the yield curve. With the most recent Fed cut, we are going to see a lowering in the dividend here. Expect this dynamic to continue in the future as well - if we do get to 3% Fed Funds by the end of 2026, as the SOFR futures market is telling us, do expect the TBIL 30-day dividend yield to move down to that level as well. Investors looking for higher yields and protection from cuts should look at SHY and NEAR.

data.title: Manulife: Buybacks And 10% Earnings Yield Make It A Buy
data.description:
Interest rate cuts can be great for borrowers but not so much for savers, as lower rates make money market funds and short-dated bonds less attractive. Such may be the case now, as the Federal Reserve cut rates by a quarter point and signaled potential for two more cuts coming this year. As such, dividend stocks that offer growing payouts and a decent yield could catch a bid from the $7 trillion of liquid assets sitting on the sidelines in money market funds.

This brings me to Manulife Financial (NYSE:MFC), which I last covered in June, highlighting its robust earnings growth and catalysts from high-margin wealth management business. While MFC’s current price of $31 isn’t far off from its 52-week high of $33, it remains attractively valued with a 10x forward PE and a 4.1% dividend yield.

In this article, I revisit MFC including recent business fundamentals, and discuss why it’s currently a ‘Buy’ for its value, income, and growth potential, so let’s get started!

Why MFC?

Manulife Financial is a large insurance and wealth management company based in Canada, with a footprint spanning Asia, Canada, and the United States. It has a 130-year history, and has evolved into a digitally enabled, globally diversified insurer and asset manager. MFC’s product portfolio includes life insurance, wealth and asset management, retirement solutions, and reinsurance businesses.

MFC’s diversification, particularly in its exposure to Asia wealth management, has resulted in robust shareholder returns. This is reflected by MFC’s 209% total return over the past 10 years. While this trails the 243% of the S&P 500 (SPY), it handily beats the 172% and 165% of peers Canadian insurance peers Sun Life Financial (SLF) and Great-West LifeCo (OTCPK:GWLIF), as shown below.

MFC continued to deliver strong momentum in Q2 2025, with APE (annualized premium equivalent) sales growth of 15% YoY. This was driven by strong contributions from Asia and the U.S. at 31% and 40%, respectively. Encouragingly, all of MFC’s insurance segments delivered over 30% growth in new business CSM (contractual service margin), which is a key indicator of future earnings power. Plus, new business value rose by a robust 20% YoY. As shown below, Asia has demonstrated strong momentum across all fronts over the past 12 months.

At the same time MFC’s Global WAM (wealth and asset management) arm delivered its seventh consecutive quarter of +20% YoY growth in pretax core earnings. Notably, net inflows reached nearly $1 billion, driven by institutional and retirement channels and core EBITDA margin expanded to 30.1%.

These strong results led to book value per share growth of 7% YoY to C$35.78, even after returning C$6.4 billion to shareholders through stock buybacks and dividends. As shown below, MFC has repurchased a significant 10% of its outstanding shares over the past 3 years.

Looking ahead, MFC is well-positioned for the fast-growing alternative asset management segment through its agreement to acquire a 75% stake in Comvest Credit Partners. It plans to have full ownership of this business in six years. Comvest is a private credit manager with $14.7 billion in AUM and will expand MFC’s already robust alternatives platform to nearly $100 billion. Synergies from this deal include MFC’s ability to leverage its 19 million clients and global distribution channels, particularly in Asia, to scale Comvest’s offerings.

Additionally, Asia remains a strong growth driver for MFC due to expanding middle-class demand for insurance and wealth products. MFC also maintains a conservative balance sheet and 96% of fixed income holdings are investment grade. This well insulates it against credit shocks.

MFC carries a strong ‘A’ credit rating from S&P and this is supported by a 136% LICAT ratio, sitting well ahead of the 100% minimum requirement set by Canada’s financial regulator. This supports MFC’s 4.1% dividend yield, which is well-protected by a 43% payout ratio, leaving plenty of retained capital for business expansion and share buybacks. The dividend also comes with an 8.6% 5-year CAGR and 11 consecutive years of growth.

Lastly, I find value in MFC at the current price of $31.03 with a forward PE of 10.7, sitting far below its historical PE of 15.5, as shown below.

At the current valuation, the market does not seem to fully appreciate MFC’s growth potential in Global WAM and upside potential in Asia. Plus, it’s getting near 10% earnings yield on share buybacks at the current price. Over the medium term, I expect for MFC to be able to achieve 7-9% EPS growth annually, including accretion from share buybacks. This, combined with the 4.1% yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation could result in double-digit total returns from here.

Risks to MFC include potential for a tougher business climate in Canada and the U.S. due to a slow job market. This is reflected by an $82 million after-tax provision for expected credit losses during Q2. Moreover, MFC’s annuities business faces longevity risks, should policyholders live significantly longer than what actuarial models predict. Plus, volatility in equity markets could reduce MFC’s fee revenue based on AUM.

Investor Takeaway

Manulife Financial presents a compelling opportunity for value and income investors. Its 4.1% dividend yield, strong capital position, and consistent buybacks offer an attractive proposition. MFC has robust momentum in its core businesses, particularly in Asia, and in and global wealth management. Its strategic move into private credit via Comvest Credit Partners expands its alternatives platform and positions it for long-term growth.

Lastly, MFC trades at a discounted 10.7x forward PE that’s well below its historical average. As such, MFC remains undervalued for its consistent performance and upside potential, making it a solid 'Buy' for income, value, and growth.
